title: Corion marinum
taxonomy: Corion marinum is classified as a type of flowering plant within the family Amaranthaceae, which belongs to the order Caryophyllales. It is native to the Mediterranean region and has been naturalized in other parts of the world. This classification is based on its botanical characteristics and genetic relationships with other plants. Corion marinum is a member of the genus Coris, which is a group of annual plants. Further classification includes its species rank and other taxonomic details.
uses: Corion marinum, also known as Water Avens, has been used in traditional medicine for its potential anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. Its leaves and roots are said to have been employed to treat various health conditions, including fever, sore throats, and digestive issues. The plant has been used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures that are believed to have antiseptic and antiviral properties. Additionally, Corion marinum is thought to have been used to treat skin conditions such as acne, wounds, and eczema. However, its effectiveness and safety for human consumption have not been extensively scientifically studied.
benefits: Corion marinum, also known as water avens, has been used in traditional medicine to treat various health conditions. Its roots and leaves contain bioactive compounds with potential antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties. Research suggests that Corion marinum may help alleviate symptoms of respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and asthma. The plant contains bioactive compounds such as flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have been shown to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities. parts: Corion marinum, also known as water hemlock, is considered one of the most toxic plants in North America. The entire plant is highly toxic, but the roots and seeds are particularly hazardous to humans and animals. The roots contain a high concentration of cicutoxin, a toxic compound that can cause seizures, muscle spasms, and respiratory failure. The seeds are also highly toxic, and can be fatal if ingested. Any part of the plant, if ingested, should be treated as a medical emergency.
preparations: Corion marinum, also known as water avens or avens, has been used in traditional herbal medicine for its potential therapeutic benefits. The plant's roots, leaves, and flowers have been used to make herbal preparations, such as infusions, decoctions, and tinctures. These preparations have been traditionally used to treat various health issues, including digestive problems, skin conditions, and respiratory issues. The exact mechanisms by which Corion marinum exerts its effects are not fully understood and require further research. More studies are needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of Corion marinum preparations for human use.
side_effects: Corion marinum, also known as water hemlock, is highly toxic and can cause severe side effects if ingested. Ingestion of the plant can lead to symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain, and can also cause neurological symptoms like tremors, seizures, and respiratory failure. In severe cases, it can be fatal. There is no known antidote for Corion marinum poisoning, and treatment typically involves supportive care to manage symptoms. Long-term exposure to the plant's toxins can also cause liver and kidney damage.
